item.title: Androidń│╗ń╗¤ÕÉ»ÕŖ©µĄüń©ŗ
item.description:
Androidń│╗ń╗¤ÕÉ»ÕŖ©µĄüń©ŗ

Androidń│╗ń╗¤Õ¤║õ║ÄLinux’╝īÕøĀµŁżÕÉ»ÕŖ©AndroidńÜäń¼¼õĖĆµŁźµś»ÕÉ»ÕŖ©LinuxŃĆé

Linux kernel startup

Bootloader

Bootloaderµś»Õ£©Ķ«Īń«Śµ£║µł¢ÕĄīÕģźÕ╝Åń│╗ń╗¤ÕÉ»ÕŖ©µŚČĶ┐ÉĶĪīńÜäõĖĆµ«ĄÕ░Åń©ŗÕ║ÅŃĆéÕģČõĖ╗Ķ”üÕŖ¤ĶāĮµś»ÕłØÕ¦ŗÕī¢ń│╗ń╗¤ńĪ¼õ╗ČÕÆīÕŖĀĶĮĮµōŹõĮ£ń│╗ń╗¤ŃĆéBootloaderÕ£©ńöĄµ║ÉÕ╝ĆÕÉ»ÕÉÄńö▒ń│╗ń╗¤ńÜäÕø║õ╗Č’╝łBIOS/UEFI’╝ēĶ░āńö©’╝īÕ╣ČÕćåÕżćń│╗ń╗¤ńĪ¼õ╗Č’╝łÕ”éÕåģÕŁśµÄ¦ÕłČÕÖ©ŃĆüÕżäńÉåÕÖ©ŃĆüÕż¢Ķ«Š’╝ēõ╗źÕÅŖĶĮ»õ╗ČńÄ»Õóā’╝īõĮ┐ÕģČÕćåÕżćÕźĮÕŖĀĶĮĮÕ╣ČÕÉ»ÕŖ©µōŹõĮ£ń│╗ń╗¤ŃĆé

  1. ńöĄµ║ÉÕ╝ĆÕÉ»’╝ÜµīēõĖŗÕ╝Ćµ£║ķö«’╝īõĖ╗µØ┐ÕÅæķĆüõ┐ĪÕÅĘń╗ÖńöĄµ║ÉŃĆéĶ«ŠÕżćńöĄµ║ÉÕ╝ĆÕÉ»ÕÉÄ’╝īÕø║õ╗Č’╝łBIOS/UEFI’╝ēµē¦ĶĪīĶć¬µŻĆ’╝łPower-On Self Test, POST’╝ēÕ╣ČĶ»åÕł½ÕÉ»ÕŖ©Ķ«ŠÕżćŃĆé
  2. ÕŖĀĶĮĮBootloader’╝ÜÕø║õ╗Čõ╗ÄķóäĶ«ŠńÜäÕÉ»ÕŖ©Ķ«ŠÕżć’╝łÕ”éńĪ¼ńøśŃĆüSSDŃĆüUSBķ®▒ÕŖ©ÕÖ©µł¢ńĮæń╗£’╝ēõĖŁÕŖĀĶĮĮBootloaderÕł░ÕåģÕŁśõĖŁµē¦ĶĪīŃĆé
  3. ńĪ¼õ╗ČÕłØÕ¦ŗÕī¢’╝ÜBootloaderķģŹńĮ«Õ┐ģĶ”üńÜäńĪ¼õ╗ČĶĄäµ║É’╝īÕ”éĶ«ŠńĮ«ÕåģÕŁśµÄ¦ÕłČÕÖ©ŃĆüµŻĆµĄŗÕż¢ķā©ÕŁśÕé©Ķ«ŠÕżćńŁēŃĆé
  4. ÕŖĀĶĮĮµōŹõĮ£ń│╗ń╗¤’╝ÜBootloaderõ╗ÄÕŁśÕé©Ķ«ŠÕżćĶ»╗ÕÅ¢µōŹõĮ£ń│╗ń╗¤ńÜäµĀĖÕ┐āµśĀÕāÅ’╝łÕ”éLinuxńÜävmlinuz’╝īWindowsńÜäbootmgr’╝ē’╝īÕ╣ČÕ░åÕģČÕŖĀĶĮĮÕł░ÕåģÕŁśõĖŁŃĆé
  5. õ╝ĀķĆÆµÄ¦ÕłČµØā’╝ÜõĖĆµŚ”µōŹõĮ£ń│╗ń╗¤µĀĖÕ┐āĶó½ÕŖĀĶĮĮõĖöÕłØÕ¦ŗÕī¢Õ«īµłÉ’╝īBootloaderÕ░åµÄ¦ÕłČµØāõ╝ĀķĆÆń╗ÖµōŹõĮ£ń│╗ń╗¤’╝īÕ«īµłÉÕÉ»ÕŖ©Ķ┐ćń©ŗŃĆé

Linux kernel

kernelÕżäńÉåµēĆµ£ēµōŹõĮ£ń│╗ń╗¤Ķ┐øń©ŗ’╝īõŠŗÕ”éÕåģÕŁśń«ĪńÉå’╝łMM’╝ēŃĆüõ╗╗ÕŖĪĶ░āÕ║”’╝łschedule’╝ēŃĆüI/OŃĆüĶ┐øń©ŗķŚ┤ķĆÜõ┐Ī’╝łIPC’╝ēÕÆīµĢ┤õĖ¬ń│╗ń╗¤µÄ¦ÕłČŃĆékernelÕÉ»ÕŖ©ÕłåõĖ║õĖżõĖ¬ķśČµ«Ą’╝Ü

Stage 1

Õ£©ń¼¼õĖĆķśČµ«Ą’╝īkernelĶ┐śÕÅ¬µś»õĖĆõĖ¬ÕÄŗń╝®ķĢ£ÕāÅµ¢ćõ╗Č’╝īµēĆõ╗źń¼¼õĖĆķśČµ«ĄńÜäÕĘźõĮ£Õ░▒µś»Õ░åkernelÕÄŗń╝®µ¢ćõ╗ČÕŖĀĶĮĮĶ┐øÕåģÕŁśŃĆékernelõ╝ÜĶ┐øĶĪīĶć¬Ķ¦ŻÕÄŗ’╝łÕ£©ARM64µ×Čµ×äõĖŁ’╝īkernelńö▒bootloaderĶ¦ŻÕÄŗ’╝ē’╝īÕ╣ČÕłØÕ¦ŗÕī¢õĖĆõ║øÕ¤║µ£¼ÕŖ¤ĶāĮ’╝īõŠŗÕ”éÕ¤║µ£¼ÕåģÕŁśń«ĪńÉåŃĆüµ£ĆÕ¤║µ£¼ńÜäńĪ¼õ╗ČÕłØÕ¦ŗÕī¢ŃĆéÕ¤║µ£¼Õ«īµłÉõ║åÕ¤║ńĪĆńÜäCPUµ×Čµ×äńøĖÕģ│ńÜäÕĘźõĮ£’╝īõĖ║ń¼¼õ║īķśČµ«ĄķØ×µ×Čµ×äńøĖÕģ│ńÜäÕÉ»ÕŖ©Ķ┐ćń©ŗÕüÜÕćåÕżćŃĆé

Stage 2

Õ£©ń¼¼õ║īķśČµ«Ą’╝łstart_kernel()’╝ē’╝īkernelõ╝ÜĶ«ŠńĮ«õĖŁµ¢ŁÕżäńÉå’╝łõĖŁµ¢ŁÕÉæķćÅĶĪ©’╝ē’╝īÕåģÕŁśńøĖÕģ│Ķ«ŠńĮ«’╝īµīéĶĮĮÕåģÕŁśõĖ║initramfs’╝łinitial ram file system’╝ēµł¢initrd’╝łinitial RAM disk’╝ē’╝īõĮ£õĖ║õĖ┤µŚČµ¢ćõ╗Čń│╗ń╗¤ŃĆé

initrdĶ«®kernelĶāĮÕż¤Õ«īÕģ©ÕÉ»ÕŖ©Õ╣ČÕŖĀĶĮĮķ®▒ÕŖ©’╝łõĖŹķ£ĆĶ”üõŠØĶĄ¢ÕģČõ╗¢ńĪ¼õ╗ČĶ«ŠÕżć’╝ē’╝īinitrdÕīģÕÉ½õ║åÕż¦ķā©ÕłåÕÅ»ĶāĮõ╝Üķ£ĆĶ”üńÜäķ®▒ÕŖ©’╝īĶ«®kernelµø┤Õ░ÅŃĆéinitramfsµś»µŚ®µ£¤ńö©µłĘń®║ķŚ┤’╝īńö©õ║ÄµŻĆµĄŗÕ╣ČÕŖĀĶĮĮķ£ĆĶ”üńÜäĶ«ŠÕżćķ®▒ÕŖ©Õł░ńö©µłĘń®║ķŚ┤ńÜäµ¢ćõ╗Čń│╗ń╗¤ŃĆéÕ«īµłÉÕÉÄ’╝īõĖ┤µŚČµ¢ćõ╗Čń│╗ń╗¤Ķó½ń£¤µŁŻńÜäńö©µłĘń®║ķŚ┤ÕÅ¢õ╗ŻŃĆéÕåģÕŁśĶó½ķćŖµöŠ’╝īkernelÕ░åõ╝ÜÕÉ»ÕŖ©PID=1ńÜäńö©µłĘń®║ķŚ┤Ķ┐øń©ŗinitŃĆé

Idle

start_kernel()->
    rest_init()->
        cpu_startup_entry()                  PID=0

Idleµś»PID=0ńÜäĶ┐øń©ŗŃĆéÕ£©start_kernel()õĖŁ’╝īń¼¼õĖĆõĖ¬µē¦ĶĪīńÜäÕćĮµĢ░set_task_stack_end_magic(&init_task)Õ░åĶć¬Ķ║½Ķ┐øń©ŗõĖÄinit_taskķōŠµÄźĶĄĘµØź’╝īĶĆīinit_taskµś»õĖĆõĖ¬Õģ©Õ▒ĆÕÅśķćÅ’╝īÕĘ▓ń╗ÅĶó½ÕłØÕ¦ŗÕī¢ŃĆéõ╣¤Õ░▒µś»Ķ»┤’╝īµŁżµŚČµē¦ĶĪīstart_kernel()ńÜäĶ┐øń©ŗµś»ń│╗ń╗¤õĖŁń¼¼õĖĆõĖ¬Ķ┐øń©ŗ’╝īPIDĶó½ÕłØÕ¦ŗÕī¢õĖ║0’╝īõ╣¤µś»Õö»õĖĆõĖĆõĖ¬µ▓Īµ£ēķĆÜĶ┐ćforkµł¢kernel_threadÕłøÕ╗║ńÜäĶ┐øń©ŗŃĆéÕ£©start_kernel()ńÜäµ£ĆÕÉÄ’╝īķĆÜĶ┐ćcpu_startup_entry()ÕÅśõĖ║idleĶ┐øń©ŗ’╝īCPUÕ£©ń®║ĶĮ¼µŚČĶ┐ÉĶĪīµŁżĶ┐øń©ŗŃĆé

Init

start_kernel()->
    rest_init()->
        user_mode_thread(kernel_init)->      Õ»╗µēŠinitńÜäµ¢ćõ╗Č
            kernel_clone()->
                copy_process()->             PID=1’╝īĶÖĮńäČµŁżµŚČPID=1’╝īõĮåµś»õŠØµŚ¦µś»ÕåģµĀĖń║┐ń©ŗ
                    run_init_process()->
                        kernel_execve()->
                            bprm_execve()->
                                exec_binprm()->
                                    search_binary_handler()->
                                        fmt->load_binary()->
                                            load_elf_binary()/load_flat_binary()->
                                                start_thread()->
                                                    start_thread_common()    Ķ┐ÖķćīĶĮ¼õĖ║ńö©µłĘµĆüĶ┐øń©ŗ

Initµś»ń¼¼õĖĆõĖ¬Ķó½kernelÕÉ»ÕŖ©ńÜäńö©µłĘń®║ķŚ┤Ķ┐øń©ŗ’╝īµēĆõ╗źPIDõĖ║1ŃĆéĶ┐Öµś»õĖĆõĖ¬ÕÉÄÕÅ░Ķ┐øń©ŗ’╝īõĖŹõĖÄńö©µłĘńø┤µÄźõ║żõ║ÆŃĆéInitõ╝ÜÕ╝ĢÕ»╝ńö©µłĘń®║ķŚ┤’╝łµīéĶĮĮµ¢ćõ╗Čń│╗ń╗¤’╝īÕÉ»ÕŖ©ÕģČÕ«āń©ŗÕ║Å’╝ēŃĆéInitµŗźµ£ēÕżÜõĖ¬runlevel’╝łõĖĆĶł¼µś»7õĖ¬’╝īõĖ║0~6’╝ē’╝īõĖŹÕÉīńÜärunlevelÕ»╣Õ║öinitõĖŹÕÉīńÜäĶ┐ÉĶĪīńŖČµĆüŃĆéAndroidÕ«×ńÄ░ńÜäinitńÜäÕģĘõĮōÕŖ¤ĶāĮÕ”éõĖŗ’╝Ü

item.description:
AOSP directories intro

AOSP directories intro

The ŌĆŗŌĆŗŌĆŗAndroid Open Source Project (AOSP)ŌĆŗŌĆŗŌĆŗ is a large-scale project, and its source code is organized into various directories. Each directory plays a specific role in the Android platform’s development. Below is a high-level overview of the main directories within AOSP:

1. art/

  • Purpose: Contains the Android Runtime (ART), which is responsible for executing Android applications. ART is the runtime that replaced the Dalvik VM, supporting Ahead-of-Time (AOT) and Just-in-Time (JIT) compilation.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • compiler/: Handles the AOT/JIT compilation of bytecode.
    • runtime/: Core runtime engine that executes the compiled code.

2. bionic/

  • Purpose: Implements the Android version of the standard C library (libc), optimized for low memory and performance in embedded systems. It also includes the dynamic linker (libdl) and the math library (libm).
  • Key subdirectories:
    • libc/: The C standard library.
    • libm/: The math library.
    • linker/: The dynamic linker responsible for loading shared libraries.

3. bootable/

  • Purpose: Contains components for the boot process of Android devices, such as the recovery system.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • recovery/: Source for the Android recovery system, used for flashing, updating, or resetting the device.

4. build/

  • Purpose: This directory manages the build system for Android, containing scripts and configuration files that define how the entire Android platform is compiled.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • core/: Core makefiles and scripts for the build process.
    • soong/: Newer build system based on Soong, which is gradually replacing the older make system.

5. cts/

  • Purpose: Houses the Compatibility Test Suite (CTS), used to ensure that devices comply with AndroidŌĆÖs compatibility requirements.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • tests/: The actual test cases for various components of Android.

6. dalvik/

  • Purpose: Contains the legacy Dalvik VM, the runtime environment that was used before ART. Although no longer actively used, this directory holds the legacy code for reference.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • vm/: The Dalvik virtual machine.

7. development/

  • Purpose: Contains tools and utilities for Android developers, such as sample code, development tools, and debugging utilities.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • tools/: Various development tools and scripts.
    • samples/: Sample applications and framework examples.

8. device/

  • Purpose: Contains device-specific configurations, typically used to define device hardware characteristics, build configurations, and board support packages (BSPs).
  • Key subdirectories:
    • ŌĆŗgeneric/ŌĆŗ: Generic configurations for reference devices.
    • ŌĆŗgoogle/ŌĆŗ: Device-specific configurations for Google-branded devices like Pixel.
    • ŌĆŗqcom/ŌĆŗ: Qcom defined SSI
    • ŌĆŗxiaomi/missi/ŌĆŗ: Extend SSI of xiaomi from Qcom SSI
    • ŌĆŗxiaomi/xxx/product/ŌĆŗ: xxx’s product

9. external/

  • Purpose: Includes external open-source libraries and projects that Android depends on but are not natively developed by the Android team.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • Multiple external libraries like openssl/, sqlite/, webkit/, etc.

10. frameworks/

  • Purpose: Contains the core framework that defines AndroidŌĆÖs application model and essential system services. ItŌĆÖs one of the most critical directories in AOSP.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • base/: Core Android framework, containing essential services like Activity Manager, Package Manager, etc.
    • av/: Audio/Video framework, handles media playback, recording, and camera access.
    • native/: Native system framework that handles low-level services such as SurfaceFlinger for rendering.
    • opt/: Optional framework components such as Bluetooth and telephony.

11. hardware/

  • Purpose: Contains hardware abstraction layers (HALs) for different devices, allowing Android to interact with hardware like cameras, sensors, and radios.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • libhardware/: Generic hardware abstraction library.
    • interfaces/: HAL interfaces for different hardware components.

12. kernel/ (Optional, not always part of AOSP)

  • Purpose: Contains device-specific Linux kernel sources. Not every AOSP repository includes the kernel, as it is typically distributed separately.
  • Note: This directory may vary depending on the device and is often provided by the device manufacturer.

13. libcore/

  • Purpose: Provides core libraries for Android, including the Java class libraries (like java.util, java.lang) and APIs that Android applications depend on.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • ojluni/: OpenJDK-based libraries used by Android for its Java runtime.

14. packages/

  • Purpose: Contains default system applications and services that come with Android, like the launcher, calendar, contacts, and messaging apps.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • apps/: Core Android applications (e.g., Dialer, Settings).
    • providers/: Content providers (e.g., ContactsProvider, MediaProvider).
    • services/: System services that interact with apps and hardware.

15. platform_testing/

  • Purpose: Contains test suites and testing frameworks to ensure that the platform functions as expected. This is vital for regression testing.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • tests/: Test cases for platform functionality.
    • cts/: Compatibility Test Suite cases for platform compliance.

16. prebuilts/

  • Purpose: Houses precompiled binaries and libraries used during the build process, such as toolchains, compilers, and prebuilt SDK components.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • sdk/: Prebuilt SDK libraries for Android development.
    • gcc/: Prebuilt toolchains for compiling Android.

17. sdk/

  • Purpose: Contains components for the Android Software Development Kit (SDK), such as the Android emulator and tools for application development.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • tools/: Android SDK tools like adb and emulator.

18. system/

  • Purpose: Contains the core system components needed for AndroidŌĆÖs runtime and basic system services.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • core/: Core system services like init, logcat, adb.
    • sepolicy/: SELinux policy configuration.
    • vold/: Volume daemon for managing storage.

19. test/

  • Purpose: Contains tests for various parts of the system, particularly focusing on lower-level functionalities.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • jni/: Tests for native code (JNI).
    • suites/: Organized test suites.

20. tools/

  • Purpose: Provides a range of utilities and tools for building and debugging Android, such as the Android Debug Bridge (adb), the TraceView profiler, and others.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • adb/: Source code for the adb tool.
    • traceview/: Tools for profiling and performance analysis.

21. vendor/

  • Purpose: Contains vendor-specific code, such as proprietary drivers, HAL implementations, and device-specific configurations provided by device manufacturers.
  • Key subdirectories:
    • Vendor-specific configurations and binaries.

ŌĆŹ

item.title: Õł®ńö©compile_commands.jsonÕÆīClangdń£ŗõ╗ŻńĀü
item.description:
Õł®ńö©compile_commands.jsonÕÆīClangdń£ŗõ╗ŻńĀü

https://android.googlesource.com/platform/build/soong/+/HEAD/docs/compdb.md

Compdb (compile_commands.json) Generator

Soong can generate compdb files. This is intended for use with editing tools such as YouCompleteMe and other libclang based completers.

compdb file generation is enabled via environment variable:

export SOONG_GEN_COMPDB=1
export SOONG_GEN_COMPDB_DEBUG=1

One can make soong generate a symlink to the compdb file using an environment variable:

export SOONG_LINK_COMPDB_TO=$ANDROID_HOST_OUT

You can then trigger an empty build:

make nothing

Note that if you build using mm or other limited makes with these environment variables set the compdb will only include files in included modules.
